coredata.dc:description: A graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and manganese dioxide (MnO2) co-decorated, polydopamine (PDA) capped iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH) nanorod was firstly constructed as a promising platform for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) guided tumor synergistic therapy. Each component played an irreplaceable role. Tumor microenvironment activated cascade Fenton catalytic activities and MRI capability at different stages were obtained, which can simultaneously realize diagnosis and chemodynamic therapy (CDT) for cancer. In addition, photothermal therapy (PTT) was also achieved owing to the excellent photothermal conversion efficiency of PDA and GQDs, which further improve the Fenton catalytic activities and realized synergistic therapy for cancer. The experimental results showed that only about 30% of cancer cells was killed when CDT or NIR laser alone for cancer inhibition. However, when PTT and CDT were employed simultaneously to treat the cancer cells, thetherapeuticefficiency has increased by 300% (90% of cancer cells was killed and about 10% cancer cells survived) due to combinatorial effect of PTT and CDT. Obviously, a more effective treatment of cancer could be realized using MRI guided tumor synergistic therapy with the as-prepared nanocomposites. This study provides a proof of concept of design and construction of complex nanomaterials for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

head.ce:title: Design and fabrication of cascade novel Fenton catalytic nanocomposite as theranostic agent for chemodynamic/photothermal synergistic tumor therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional and multilayer nanocomposite (manganese dioxide @graphene quantum dots@polydopamine@ iron oxyhydroxide) was firstly constructed by a wet-chemical method, which could be used as theranostic agent for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) guided chemodynamic/photothermal synergistic tumor therapy.

A graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and manganese dioxide (MnO2) co-decorated, polydopamine (PDA) capped iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH) nanorod was firstly constructed as a promising platform for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) guided tumor synergistic therapy. Each component played an irreplaceable role. Tumor microenvironment activated cascade Fenton catalytic activities and MRI capability at different stages were obtained, which can simultaneously realize diagnosis and chemodynamic therapy (CDT) for cancer. In addition, photothermal therapy (PTT) was also achieved owing to the excellent photothermal conversion efficiency of PDA and GQDs, which further improve the Fenton catalytic activities and realized synergistic therapy for cancer. The experimental results showed that only about 30% of cancer cells was killed when CDT or NIR laser alone for cancer inhibition. However, when PTT and CDT were employed simultaneously to treat the cancer cells, thetherapeuticefficiency has increased by 300% (90% of cancer cells was killed and about 10% cancer cells survived) due to combinatorial effect of PTT and CDT. Obviously, a more effective treatment of cancer could be realized using MRI guided tumor synergistic therapy with the as-prepared nanocomposites. This study provides a proof of concept of design and construction of complex nanomaterials for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer therapy faces great challenges using conventional therapeutic methods (surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy) due to its complexity and heterogeneity [1–2]. This challenge highlights the need for emerging therapeutic means to eliminate side effects and enhance the therapeutic efficiency. Fortunately, advances in nanotechnology provide opportunities for the development of novel cancer therapies. As emerging therapy strategies based on nanotechnology, CDT and PTT have received more and more attention due its many unique merits in increasing tumor specificity and decreasing side effects [3–9]. CDT is defined as in situ treatments employing the Fenton catalysts to produce high toxic hydroxyl radical in tumor sites. PTT is an emerging therapeutic method for cancer. Different from photooxidation catalysis, photoreduction catalysis (an important way to produce hydroperoxides to catalyze the decomposition of organic compounds in the presence of oxygen, light energy, and a photosensitizer) or other catalytic reaction, PTT is an emerging therapeutic method based on photothermal conversion effect (a rapid and effective form of energy conversion using photothermal agent absorb light well and convert it into heat), which could kill cancerous cells with hyperthermia generated by a photothermal agent under light irradiation [7,9–14]. It is clear that Fenton catalysts (CDT) or photothermal agents (PTT) play important role in CDT and PTT [15–18]. Various nanomaterials including carbon nanomaterials, noble metal nanoparticles, metal compound nanostructures and polymer nanoplatforms were prepared and used as Fenton catalysts and photothermal agents [19–22]. However, sometimes, it is difficult to achieve ideal therapeutic outcomes even with these new methods because of the lack of real-time monitoring. Designing and constructing complex nanocomposites as “theranostic nanoagents” is necessary, which could realize imaging guided tumor therapy to achieve ideal therapeutic outcomes. The choice of functional components and the form of assembly of “theranostic nanoagents” is key to realize the integration of diagnosis and treatment.

Iron (Fe) and manganese (Mn)-based nanomaterials are the most explored components for “theranostic nanoagents” due to their enhanced MRI performance and outstanding Fenton catalytic activities [23–30]. Fe3+/Fe2+ and Mn4+/Mn2+ can trigger the Fenton reaction in tumor microenvironment (TME) and generate massive ·OH for CDT, which will induce ferroptosis of cancer cells with the accumulation of lethal reactive oxygen species (ROS) [28–31]. FeOOH and MnO2 are particularly attractive in biomedical fields due to excellent Fenton catalytic activity and MRI capacity [27–29,32,33]. However, FeOOH or MnO2 alone usually cannot realize the desired diagnostic and therapeutic effect due to their inherent limitation. The combination of MnO2 with FeOOH in one nanomaterial is a preferential way to solve the problems. Mn4+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ was produced sequentially in TME when MnO2 and FeOOH were injected and concentrated in the tumor. A TME activated and cascade Fenton catalytic reaction can be achieved because Mn4+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ works at different times and have different catalytic rates. This is difficult to achieve with a single metal ion catalytic reaction. Furthermore, to produce Mn4+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+, excessive glutathione (GSH) was consumed and part of endogenous H2O2 decomposed into O2, which could regulate TME and alleviate tumor hypoxia [34,35]. In addition, the released Mn4+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ ions can be simultaneously used as contrast agents for enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which provides a way to monitor the detail of tumors [36–38]. Therefore, elaborate design of nanocomposites including FeOOH and MnO2 offers tremendous prospects for the integration of diagnosis and treatment of cancer therapy.

Though CDT is an attractive method for cancer therapy, it is sometimes difficult to achieve the desired effect of removal of the tumor due to the insufficient endogenous H2O2 and inappropriate intracellular pH. Combined PTT and CDT is reported as a good way to solve the problem [39–41]. Unfortunately, almost no photothermal conversion performance was found in FeOOH and MnO2. Novel photothermal conversion agents with high efficiency were needed. Featured with high photothermal conversion efficiency, PDA and GQDs could efficiently convert light into harmless heat energy, which could be used as excellent photothermal agents [42–48]. PDA has similar structure as naturally existing melanin, which is very suitable for biological applications due to its biocompatibility, biodegradability and excellent light protection [42–44]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of “star materials” in the field of nanomedicine, has gained much attention in biomedical areas due to their tunable photoluminescence (PL) property, excellent photostability, good biocompatibility, high photothermal conversion efficiency and non-toxic behavior in the body [45–48]. Motivated by these significant features, GQDs are increasingly used as diagnosis and therapy for cancer [49–53]. Additionally, PDA and GQDs could be easily decorated via Michael addition or Schiff base reaction due to the existence of numerous functional groups (eg, amino- and hydroxyl-) on their surface. Combining PDA, GQDs with FeOOH and MnO2 may pave one way to realize their complementary advantages and significant synergistic effect.

In the current work, we designed and synthesized a novel graphene quantum dot (GQD) and manganese dioxide (MnO2) co-decorated, polydopamine (PDA) capped iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH) rod-like nanocomposite. As shown in Scheme 1 , these four components are organically combined in harness other than messy mixed. Firstly, uniform FeOOH nanorods (Fes) was synthesized by a novel fluoride ion assisted hydrothermal route. Then, well-proportioned PDA was coated on the surface of Fes by in situ polymeric reaction of dopamine monomer to form FeOOH@PDA (FePs). Next, GQDs were decorated on the surface of FeOOH@PDA by electrostatic interaction to prepare FeOOH@PDA@GQDs (FePGs). Finally, FeOOH@PDA@GQDs@MnO2 nanocomposites (FePGMns) were obtained by in situ redox reaction of KMnO4 on the surface of FePGs. The as-prepared FePGMns possessed a variety of outstanding performance including photothermal performance, TME-responsive Fenton catalytic activities and magnetic resonance imaging capability. After accumulation at the tumor site employing the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, the as-prepared FePGMns can be gradually degraded into metal ions (Mn4+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+), PDA and GQDs in TME. On one hand, the released metal ions, PDA and GQDs were demonstrated to be a highly efficient Fenton-like agent that triggered cascade novel Fenton catalytic reaction for persistent CDT for cancer, which was discussed seldomly in early reports. And Mn4+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ also enhanced MRI performance and used as MRI diagnosis agent for cancer. On the other hand, PDA and GQDs acted as photothermal conversion agents, which could convert NIR irradiation into heat and realize PTT for cancer. Obviously, the as-prepared FePGMns can act as a novel “theranostic nanoagent”, which will realize MRI guided synergistic therapy for cancer. This work opens up a new access to design and synthesize novel complex nanohybrids, which would definitely expand the application of nanocomposites.

Iron nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), ammonium fluoride dihydrate (NH4F·2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), permanganate (KMnO4), dopamine hydrochloride potassium (DA·HCl) were all analytically pure (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). N doped graphene quantum dots (denoted as GQDs) (XFNANO Co., Ltd, Nanjing, China), propidium iodide (PI), calcein-AM 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, 97%), (Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) were used as received without further purification. Cell culture materials including culture medium and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from KeyGEN BioTECH Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Rabbit monoclonal antibodies (Abs) against glutathione peroxidase 4 antibody [EPNCIR144] (ab125066) was purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). Mouse monoclonal antibodies against β-actin (#66009–1-lg) was bought from Proteintech.

1 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 10 mmol NH4F·2H2O was successively added into 24 mL deionized water with magnetic stirring. Then, 2 mmol NaOH was added in above solution with stirring. After 10 min, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave of 30 mL capacity. Next, the autoclave was sealed and heated at 120 °C for 10 h. The resulting products were obtained via centrifugation and washed several times after the reaction system is cooled down. For sake of charactering the samples in following experiments, part of Fes was vacuum-freeze dried, others were re-dispersed in water.

Briefly, the FeOOH nanorods (40 mg) were added to the mixed solution of tris buffer (10 mL, pH = 8.0) and ethanol (20 mL), and stirred for 10 min on a magnetic stirrer. Then, 10 mL of dopamine hydrochloride aqueous solution (containing 20.1 mg DA-HCl) was added with stirring overnight. The as-prepared FePs were treated with the same method as Fes.

4 mL GQDs aqueous solution (1 mg⋅mL−1) were added in 20 mL FePs ethanol dispersion solution (containing 20 mg FePs) at 60 °C and oscillated for 6 h. Then the as-prepared FePGs were obtained using the same method as Fes.

4 mL FePGs dispersion solution (containing 20 mg FePGs) was added into 36 mL KMnO4 aqueous solution (0.01 M) and stirred for 24 h. Then, the precipitates were obtained via centrifugation and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, respectively. Then the as-prepared FePGMns were treated with the same method as Fes.

The formation of the as-prepared nanocomposites was characterized by different microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, TecnaiTM G2 Spirit TWIN, America), field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM, FEI, Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN, America), UV–vis (AOE, UV-1902, China), FTIR (Thermo Fisher, NICOLET 5700, America), Raman Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, DXRxi, America) and X-ray photoelectron spectrum system (Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB 250Xi, America) were employed in the experiments. An inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometer (Agilent, 725 ICP-OES, America) was used to detect the Mn element concentrations in FePGMns buffer solution. A zeta potential instrument (Malvern, NICOMP 380ZLS, UK) was employed to study the Zeta potential of the as-prepared samples. The photothermal conversion performance of the as-prepared nanocomposites were acquired employing an IR camera (Fotric, S225, USA) and NIR (Laserwave, 808 nm, China) laser instrument. A 3.0 T MR system (GE, Discovery 750w, America) was used to characterize the magnetic resonance imaging performance of FePGMns. Microplate Reader (Bio-rad, Mode 680, America) and confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica, STELLARIS 5, Germany) was used in cells and animal experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effect evaluation in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the photothermal effect of the as-prepared nanocomposites, the dispersing solution of different nanomaterials (Fes, FePs, FePGs and FePGMns) were irradiated by 808 nm NIR laser (Beijing Laserwave OptoElectronics Technology Co., Ltd). Deionized (DI) water was used as a control. The photothermal images and temperature variation in all samples were obtained by an IR camera (Fotric, S225). And the other photothermal experimental results of FePGMns (different concentrations of dispersing solution, several “laser on–off” cycles and conversion efficiency) were also acquired by IR camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity (Enzymatic-like activity, GSH consumption and Fenton catalytic activity)
ce:section.ce:para: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the as-prepared nanocomposites were carried out by catalyzing 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). For peroxidase-like catalytic activity, various nanocomposites (50 μg·mL−1) were reacted with TMB (1 mM) in presence of H2O2 (50 mM) in 3 mL buffer solution (pH 5.0). Ultraviolet absorption at 652 nm was investigated by the UV–vis spectra. Pure TMB solution (1 mM) was used as a control. For catalase-like catalytic properties, 40 mL PBS solution (pH 5.0) containing FePGMns (5 μg·mL−1, 10 μg·mL−1, and 20 μg·mL−1) and H2O2 (10 mM) were chosen, respectively. Then, the amount of O2 generation was detected every 10 s employing a portable dissolved oxygen meter (JPBJ-608). The Fenton catalytic experiments of the as-prepared nanocomposites were carried out by degrading methylene blue (MB) in presence of H2O2. Various nanocomposites (50 μg⋅mL−1) were added into 4 mL mixed aqueous solution (MB, 10 μg⋅mL−1; NaHCO3, 25 mM; H2O2, 10 mM) and oscillated at 37 °C for 30 min. The catalytic process was monitored by UV–vis spectra. GSH consumption capacity of FePGMns was also investigated using the conventional 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) assay. Typically, various concentrations of FePGMns were added into the mixture of GSH solution (0.5 mL, 6 mM) and dibasic sodium phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (0.5 mL, pH 5.0) and then incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. DTNB solution (50 μL, 12 mg⋅mL−1) was added into the above solution under continuous shaking. After centrifugation and dilution, the UV–vis was used to determine the absorbance of the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: T1-MRI Performance in vitro and vivo
ce:section.ce:para: T1-MRI properties and T1 relaxation time were measured on a GE MR system. FePGMns with various Mn concentrations were dispersed into a 0.5 mL eppendorf tube at various pH. 10 mM GSH was introduced into FePGMns dispersed solution to verify their TME responsive property. In vitro, after incubating with FePGMns (200 μg⋅mL−1, 12 h), cancer cells (HCT116) were treated for MRI. MR experiments in vivo were also carried out. Tumor-bearing mouse were intravenously injected with FePGMns (100 μL, 1.5 mg⋅mL−1) and used for MR for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity test and therapy efficacy in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: GES 1 cells (human gastric mucosal epithelial cell line) were seeded into 96-well plates and incubated with RPMI-1640 medium (100 μL) for 24 h. Then FePGMns with different concentrations were added into cells plates and was incubated for 12 h or 24 h. Next, fresh CCK8 assay was added and incubated for another 3 h. Finally, cytotoxicity of the cells was obtained by optical density (OD) values at 450 nm on a microplate reader. The OD values of FePGMns RPMI-1640 solution under same condition was also measured. The viability of the cancer cells (HCT 116 and SKOV3) was also investigated using the same procedures. The therapy efficacy for cancer cell was calculated with and without near-infrared NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 2 W⋅cm−2, 3 min). To further evaluate intuitively cell viability of the as-prepared nanocomposites, calcein-AM and propidium iodide were employed to co-stain the cells to distinguish live and dead cells. Similar to the procedures of cytotoxicity test, cancer cells were seeded into 6-well plates and incubated with DMEM medium for 12 h. Then fresh DMEM medium containing FePGMns were used to treat cells. After incubation for another 12 h, the cells with and without NIR irradiation and co-stained with propidium iodide (PI) and calcein-AM for 10 min. Finally, the images of live and dead cells were acquired by fluorescence microscopy.

Cells were planted in six-well plates and incubated for 12 h. Then, the cell culture medium was replaced with fresh medium containing the as-prepared nanocomposites (Fes, FePs, FePGs and FePGMns). After incubation for 30 min, the cells were treated with DCFH-DA solution (1 mL, 10 μmol⋅L-1) to detect ROS. The fluorescence intensity in cells was observed by CLSM. For photothermal enhanced group, cells were irradiated by NIR laser for 3 min after co-incubation with as-prepared nanocomposites (Fes, FePs, FePGs and FePGMns). Then the cells were treated with DCFH-DA solution (1 mL, 10 μmol⋅L-1), which was investigated by CLSM.

The samples derived from cells were lysed in whole cell lysates (KeyGEN), then separated by electrophoresis and transferred to nitrocellulose filter membranes (ExCell Bio, Shanghai, China). Proteins were detected using primary Abs at a concentration of 1:1000 Gel bands were observed by the enhanced chemiluminescence Odyssey CLX laser imaging scan system (LI-COR, NE) and quantified by Image J software. Data were presented as means ± SD from three independent immunoblotting assays.

All animal (BALB/c nude mice, 18–20 g, female, 6–8 weeks) procedures comply with institutional animal regulations (the National Institutes of Health guide for the care and use of Laboratory animals (NIH Publications No. 8023, revised 1978)). To obtain tumor-bearing mice, HCT116 cells (1 × 107) were administrated into the right flank of the mice. When the size of the tumor grows about 100 mm3, all tumor model of HCT116 tumor-bearing mice were established. The tumor model of SKOV3 tumor-bearing mice was obtained using a same method.

All HCT116 tumor-bearing mice randomly divided into four groups (n = 5): (i) injecting with normal saline (NS); (ii) injecting with NS and NIR irradiation (NIR laser, 2 W⋅cm−2, 5 min) (iii) injecting with FePGMns (100 μL, 1.5 mg⋅mL−1) (iv) injecting with FePGMns (100 μL, 1.5 mg⋅mL−1) and NIR irradiation (NIR laser, 2 W⋅cm−2, 5 min). Then, the tumor volume and appearance of all the mice were gauged every 4 days for 28 days to estimate the efficiency of in vivo tumor treatment. In the process of treatment, the photothermal imaging and temperature distributions of ii and iv groups were obtained on a Fotric S225 infrared thermal camera. SKOV3 tumor-bearing mice were also investigated according to the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histological staining and evaluation of blood biochemical index
ce:section.ce:para: The representative tumor-bearing mice from different groups after cancer treatment (28 days) were sacrificed. Then, main organs and tumors were removed and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for hematoxylin and eosin staining (H&E). The results could be obtained on an optical microscope. The blood biochemical indicator was measured.

The quantitative data analysis was manifested as mean ± SD. More than three samples of statistical significance were collected. Statistical differences were evaluated by one-way ANOVA (Tukey’s post hoc test) or Student’s t test. The statistical significance was speculated at a value of *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

TEM, FETEM and EDS-mapping were employed to characterize the morphology of the as-prepared nanocomposites. TEM showed that the obtained Fes were composed of rod-like shape, and the length and width of the nanorods were about 30–50 nm and 5 nm, respectively in Fig. 1 a. After polymerization of DA (Fig. 1b), the size and morphology of the nanorods have not apparently changed, except that a light-white coating was found on the surface of nanorods, indicating the formation of FePs. Then, scattered and fuzzy nanodots can be seen in FePGs, which is due to low contrast of GQDs in Fig. 1c. Finally, an obvious MnO2 layer was formed, proving successful preparation of FePGMns in Fig. 1d. The EDS spectrum of FePGMns further confirmed the existence of C, N, O, Fe and Mn elements in Fig. 1e, which could be also seen in HAADF images and EDS mapping results in Fig. 1f (Fe-blue; N-orange; C-red; Mn-green; O-yellow).

To further prove the existence of each component in FePGMns, UV–vis spectra, Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), Raman spectra and zeta potential of the as-prepared nanocomposites were measured in Fig. 2 . There is not NIR adsorption for FeOOH in Fig. 2a. After introduction of PDA and GQDs, broad absorptions ranging from 400 nm to 1000 nm were found, which indicated that FePs and FePGs could be as potential photothermal agents. And an enhanced absorption ranging from 400 nm to 600 nm was observed for FePGMns, which is characteristic absorption of manganese oxide. FTIR spectra of the as-prepared nanocomposites was also performed in Fig. 2b. The characteristic absorption bands located at 1627, 1397 and 885 cm−1 are the stretching vibration of Fe-O of FeOOH [48]. Obvious peaks at 1623 cm−1 (stretching vibration of aromatic ring), 1479 cm−1 (shearing vibration of N-H), 1285 cm−1 (stretching vibration of phenolic C − O) in FePs were observed, which supported the presence of PDA on the surface of Fes [54,55]. No obvious change on vibration modes was found in FePGs, which may be due to low content of GQDs and overlap of vibration between PDA and GQDs. For FePGMns, most peaks became less obvious due to the MnO2 layer on the surface of FePGs. Raman spectra were also obtained in Fig. 2c. It can be found that peaks at 310, 406 and 488 cm−1 in Fes could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of Fe-O of FeOOH [56]. Two broad peaks centered at 1351 cm−1 and 1565 cm−1 were found, which are from PDA according to early reports [55,57]. When GQDs were decorated on the surface of FePs, the position of the two peaks is shift slightly and the intensity of peaks became strong due to the contribution of D band and G band of GQDs [58,59]. For FePGMns, a new at 635 cm−1 was observed, which are caused by the stretching vibrations Mn-O of MnO2. It is clear that MnO2 was deposited on the surface FePGs due to reduction of KMnO4 at room temperature. Zeta potential of all prepared nanomaterials was performed in Fig. 2d. The zeta potential of FeOOH is + 40.8 mV. After the polymerization of dopamine, a transformational zeta potential (–33.9 mV) was found, indicating a layer of PDA was formed on the surface of Fes. After introducing GQDs (-29.5 mV), small potential change was observed, which may be owing to the low proportion on the surface of FePs. However, when KMnO4 was in situ reduced on the surface of FePGs, obvious variation of zeta potential was found (-44.3 mV), which proved that the FePGMns was constructed successfully.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to analyze the information of the state of elements in the nanocomposites. The survey spectrum of the FePGMns shows that it was composed of elements C, N, O, Fe and Mn (Fig. 3 a). In Fig. 3b, characteristic peaks of C1s at 284.2, 287.8, 292.1 and 294.9 eV binding belong to C-C (sp2), C-N, C-C (sp3) and O-C = O, respectively, which are from PDA and GQDs in FePGMns [60,61]. In Fig. 3c, the two characteristic peaks of O1s at 529.2 eV and 530.5 eV represents the characteristic metal–oxygen bonds and –OH group of GQDs, respectively. And in Fig. 3d, two weak peaks were found centered at 398.9 and 406.6 eV, which should originate from graphitic N of PDA and GQDs, respectively [50,51]. These results further confirmed the incorporation of PDA and GQDs with FeOOH nanorods. In Fig. 3e, two characteristics peaks are at 641.6 and 653.4 eV corresponds to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, which are originated form MnO2 [62]. In Fig. 3f, two peaks of Fe 2p were observed with the binding energies of 710.5 (Fe 2p3/2) and 724.1 eV (Fe 2p1/2) illustrating that Fe(III) bonds are the main compositions in FePGMns [63]. All the results discussed above further indicated that FeOOH, PDA, GQDs and MnO2 are organically combined in harness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance of the as-prepared nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: PDA Coating and decoration of GQDs could enhance photothermal generation efficiency. The photothermal generation efficiency of the as-prepared nanocomposites was investigated. Dispersions of different nanocomposites (Fes, FePs, FePGs and FePGMns) were irradiated by NIR light and the photothermal NIR images was recorded by IR camera. With the equal concentration (200 μg⋅mL−1), FePGs showed the largest temperature change (31 °C) under NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2 W·cm−2 for 5 min) in Fig. 4 a and 4b. By comparison, temperature change of 18.8 °C (FePs), 14.5 °C (FePMns), 2.1 °C (Fes) and 2.1 °C (H2O) was observed. Obviously, FePs, FePGs and FePGMns all possessed enhanced photothermal conversion performance due to the presence of PDA and GQDs. Although the photothermal conversion performance was significantly reduced when the formation of FePGMns. MnO2 could be easily decomposed in TME, which could be proved by UV–vis and Zeta potential in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2. Furthermore, the TME-responsive quality will reduce damage to normal tissue and provide tumor targeting. In Fig. 4c, the photothermal conversion characteristics of FePGs were obvious concentration-dependent. Obviously, even at a relatively low concentration, it can also show good photothermal performance. The recycling temperature variations of the FePGs dispersion solution were measured to discuss the photothermal stability of the as-prepared photothermal agents. No noticeable temperature attenuation was seen, accounting for good photothermal stability in Fig. 4d. And the calculated photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE η) is about 41.98% according the results in Fig. 4e, 4f and Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzymatic-like catalytic activity and Fenton catalytic activity of FePGMns
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared FePGMns are expected as a superior catalyst, which could produce copious reactive oxygen radical (ROS) to kill cancer cells. The enzymatic-like catalytic activity of FePGMns was explored by catalyzing H2O2 to produce ·OH. An indicator of ·OH generation, 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was employed to monitor the peroxidase-like catalytic reaction process. It can be found that the as-prepared FePGMns had the highest enzymatic catalytic activity for TMB oxidation in all nanocomposites at the early stage, which was reflected by the change of TMB solution color and intensity of absorption peak at 652 nm in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S4a. Interestingly, when MnO2 was consumed completely, enzymatic catalytic activity of FePs and FePGs gradually dominated the TMB oxidation at the later stage in Fig. 5b and Fig. S4b. Obviously, ·OH was produced continually and a cascade catalytic activity was obtained, which proved outstanding enzymatic-like catalytic performance of the as-prepared FePGMns. Furthermore, we also found that the pH of system has obvious influence on the generation of ·OH in Fig. 5c. When in neutral condition, almost no ·OH was generated in presence of FePGMns. It is no doubt that the acid environment benefits the generation of ·OH (Fig. 5c). Excitingly, the tumor microenvironment happens to be acidic, which favored specific the production of ·OH and targeted CDT for cancer cells (Fig. 5c). However, in TME, the generated ·OH were easily reduced by the high concentration of GSH in cancer cells, which seriously reduced the efficiency of CDT. Fortunately, FePGMns could effectively eliminate superfluous GSH. The conventional 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) assay was used to evaluate the GSH consumption capacity of FePGMns. The characteristic peak of the reaction solution (around 405 nm) significantly decreased with increasing FePGMns cocentrations in Fig. 5d, indicating a good GSH depletion performance of FePGMns. Additionally, MnO2 and FeOOH was decomposed and released Mn4+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ in TME, which will trigger Fenton catalytic reaction and produced copious ·OH. MB was selected as an indicator to Fenton catalytic activity of FePGMns. It is obvious that MB was degraded by the as-prepared FePGMns under the condition of simulating TME in Fig. 5e and Fig. S5. In addition, successive O2 was also generated at the cost of consumption of GSH in TME, which will relieve tumor hypoxia and enhance syngenetic therapy efficiency in Fig. 5f and Fig. S6. In this way, a novel TME active cascade Fenton catalytic performance was realized, which is more much fascinating than that of single component-based catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and anti-cancer performance in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: As a superior photothermal conversion agent and catalyst, the as-prepared FePGMns has wide potential application in cancer therapy. Good biocompatibility is necessary. To explore the biocompatibility of FePGMns, normal human gastric mucosa GES1 cells were employed. FePGMns of different concentrations was used to incubate with GES1 cells for 12 h and 24 h in Fig. 6 a. After incubation, the cell viability was determined with a standard CCK8 colorimetric assay. It can be seen that cell viability was no<80% even at a considerably higher concentration of 400 μg·mL−1 in Fig. 6a, suggesting the good biocompatibility of the as-prepared FePGMns. To investigate the anti-cancer performance of FePGMns, colon cancer cells HCT116 and ovarian cancer cells SKOV3 were selected and treated with the nanocomposites. For colon cancer, almost all cells survived in control group without any nanocomposites in Fig. 6b. By contrast, the cell viability gradually decreased with increase of the concentration of FePGMns. Only 44% of HCT116 cells survived when the concentration of FePGMns increased to 400 μg·mL−1. The same trend was observed for ovarian cancer cells SKOV3 in Fig. S7. Obviously, the as-prepared FePGMns has some tumor inhibition performance due to their enzymatic-like catalytic activity and Fenton catalytic activity. However, the tumor suppression cannot meet the expectation to eliminate the tumors due to the limitation of CDT. To realized more effective therapy for cancer, PTT was selected employing 808 nm NIR laser irradiation on cancer cells in presence of FePGMns. Obviously, most of HCT116 and SKOV3 cells were killed after they were treated with 200 μg·mL−1 FePGMns and irradiated by 808 nm NIR for 3 min in Fig. 6c and Fig. S8, which can be ascribed to the combinatorial effect of PTT and CDT. Comparing to the early reports on FeOOH [63], the as-prepared FePGMns had more fascinating application for diversious tumors. Live (green)/dead (red) cells assay was further performed to prove excellent antitumor efficacy in Fig. 6d. Without irradiation and nanocomposites (control group), almost no dead cells were found. When the FePGMns were introduced and incubated with cells, some dead cells were observed due to inhibition effect of FePGMns on cancer cells by CDT. Almost all of cells were dead in FePGMns + NIR group, which further proved the high anti-tumor efficacy of FePGMns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of anti-tumor of FePGMns (subcellular localization, GPX4 level and intracellular ROS)
ce:section.ce:para: To exploit the mechanism of anti-tumor of FePGMns in cells. The subcellular localization of FePGMns, GPX4 level and intracellular ROS in hct116 were discussed. TEM was employed to investigate the subcellular localization of FePGMns in cells. As shown in Fig. 6e, no any nanoparticles were found in HCT116 cells (control group). However, some rod-like nanocomposties were observed in the cytoplasm of the cells in FePGMns + NIR group (Fig. 6f and 6 g), which demonstrated that the as-prepared FePGMns could be efficiently taken up by cancer cells. Then, once FePGMns entered into cells and reacted with H+, H2O2, GSH in TME, iron and manganese ions were released and the catalytic activity of FePGMns was triggered. It led to the consumption of GSH and massive ROS generation, which further induced ferroptosis of cancer cells [64,65]. This phenomenon was also further proved by TEM, GPX4 level and ROS generation experiments. Significant changes including higher membrane density and fewer ridges in mitochondria was observed after the cells were treated with FePGMns in Fig. 6f, which is one of feature of ferroptosis. The protein expression levels of GPX4 were significantly downregulated after introduction of FePGMns (FePGMns + NIR group), which is also an indicator of ferroptosis in Fig. 6h. The intracellular ROS generation was evaluated using DCFH-DA as probe in Fig. 6i. DCFH which could be converted into dichlorofluorescein (DCF) under the oxidative stress in cells. Almost no green fluorescence signals of DCF were seen the cells without FePGMns (control group), indicating the low level of ROS in cells. However, the green fluorescence signals significant enhanced in cells when the as-prepared FePGMns were incubated with cells, which proved that massive ROS was generated by catalytic reaction. Furthermore, we also found that the NIR laser irradiation further increased the intensity of fluorescence signals, proving the synergistic effect between PTT and CDT, which agreed well with the previous Fenton catalytic activity and enzyme-like activity analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TME-triggered MR imaging in vitro and vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared FePGMns was also expected as a real-time TME-responsive MR imaging agent for cancer due to the released iron and manganese ions in TME. TME-responsive MR imaging performance of FePGMns was measured in Fig. 7 . GSH and H+ benefit T1-weighted MR. The T1-weighted MR signal was weak (r1 = 0.10 mM−1 s−1 at pH 7.4) when GSH was absent in Fig. 7a and 7b. After adding GSH into FePGMns, significant enhancement of MR imaging was obtained. Furthermore, acidic environment also further enhanced MR imaging in presence of GSH (r3 = 9.88 mM−1 s−1 at pH5.0 with GSH > r2 = 9.35 mM−1 s−1 at pH 7.4 with GSH). It is clear that the excellent TME-responsive MR performance of FePGMns was achieved, which will be applied for tumor diagnosis. The MR imaging in vitro also further convinced that FePGMns was good MR imaging agent for tumor when HCT116 cells were incubated with FePGMns for 24 h in Fig. 7c. The HCT116 cells without any nanocomposites were used as control. The MRI capacity of FePGMns in vivo was also investigated in HCT116 tumor-bearing mice in Fig. 7d. Prior to injection with FePGMns, there was no significant difference between the tumor and normal tissue. However, the T1 MRI signal at tumor gradually became brighter in 6 h after injection with FePGMns in Fig. 7d, which initiated by abundant Mn2+ and Fe3+ released from FePGMns in TME. The excreted path of FePGMns from the body also was discussed. High contrast was also found in the gall bladder and liver in Fig. 7e. By comparison, almost no signal enhancement was seen in the bladder. Apparently, the clearance of the FePGMns was mainly completed by hepatobiliary system.

In view of the fact that FePGMns have CDT/PTT synergistic effect on tumor inhibition, PTT evaluation test in vivo was carried out on mouse tumor models. The universal synergistic antitumor efficacy in vivo was carried out on HCT116 tumor bearing mice and SKOV3 tumor bearing mice in Fig. 8 and Fig.S9, respectively. The mice were treated with the as-prepared nanocomposites by tail vein injection at 1st and 7st day (Fig. 8a), and NIR laser irradiation was employed. Comparing to that of the control groups (NS), the local temperature of tumor tissue in FePGMns + NIR group was raised significantly treated with FePGMns under 808 nm laser irradiation in Fig. 8b, indicating that the good photothermal conversion performance of the as-prepared FePGMns. All mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 5 per group): (i) treated with NS (control group); (ii) treated with NS and irradiated by 808 nm Laser (NS + NIR group); (iii) treated with FePGMns (FePGMns group); (iv) treated with FePGMns and irradiated by 808 nm Laser (FePGMns + NIR). Tumor growth curves and the photos of tumor after therapy was displayed in Fig. 8c-e. Obviously, the treatment of NS could not inhibit tumor growth. The FePGMns group was more efficient in tumor suppression compared to the NS groups due to the CDT effects. The strongest anti-tumor efficacy was achieved in the FePGMns + NIR group due to synergistic effect of PTT and CDT, which supported the feasibility of FePGMns as a highly efficient cancer therapeutic agent. After all groups were treated and sacrificed, photo-luminescence (PL), HE staining and Ki67 staining was also used to assess antitumor efficacy of FePGMns in Fig.S10, Fig. 8f and 8 g. Significant necrosis of tumor cells in staining section in tumor were found when the tumor was treated with FePGMns and 808 nm laser irradiation in Fig.S10 and Fig. 8f. And the results in group iv (FePGMns + NIR group) also showed much slighter Ki67 staining than that of mice in group i (NS group) (Fig. 8f and 8 g). These further confirmed that the as-prepared FePGMns inhibit the growth of cancer in vivo. For SKOV3 tumor bearing mice, the same inhibition efficiency was achieved in Fig. S9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histological analyses and blood biochemical indicator
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the biocompatibility of FePGMns in vivo, histological and analysis serum biochemical assays were carried out. The H&E-stained images of the main organs (heart, liver, lungs and kidneys) also showed that good biocompatibility of in vivo because no significant differences between healthy mice and FePGMns + NIR treated mice was found in Fig. 9 a. All the blood biochemical indexes (aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), total protein (TP), albumin (ALB), creatinine (CREA) and urea (UREA) in FePGMns treated group displayed no abnormity as compared to control group in Fig. 9b-g, no obvious deleterious effects were occurred by the introduction of FePGMns in vivo. Good biocompatibility of FePGMns in SKOV3 tumor-bearing mice was also found in Fig. S11 and S12.

In summary, quaternary nanocomposites were prepared by a wet chemical synthetic protocol, which exhibited outstanding multifunctional properties and could be used as a novel “theranostic nanoagent” for cancer treatment. Various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques were used to confirm each component assembling into nanocomposites. And the following tests showed that the as-prepared nanocomposites possessed excellent NIR photothermal performance, high catalytic activities and MR imaging capability in TME. Multi-mode imaging guided tumor synergistic therapy were realized when the as-prepared nanocomposites were employed as “theranostic nanoagent” for cancer treatment. This present platform would have broad application prospects in combined therapy and convenient imaging of nanomaterials.
